text: The surgeon begins mobilizing the root of the terminal ileal mesentery and lifts the cecum and appendix away from the retroperitoneum.
The surgeon lifts the terminal ileal mesentery off the retroperitoneum, elevating the superior mesenteric vessels over the head of the pancreas.
The surgeon facilitates mesenteric dissection along the mesenteric vessels, aiming for improved nodal clearance.

The surgeon uses two left robotic arms and one right arm, with the camera positioned in arm three. The camera is slightly elevated in the abdomen, causing difficulty for the surgeon's right hand in arm four to maneuver around the mesentery during dissection.
The duodenum is visible in the surgical field, positioned near the mesenteric vessels and the head of the pancreas.
The surgeon attempts to sweep down the mesentery during the dissection, maneuvering around the mesenteric vessels for better access.
The surgeon adjusts the approach to a medial view due to limited visibility from the current angle, repositioning instruments to improve access to the mesentery for dissection.
The surgeon visualizes the duodenum during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon creates a small window in the iliocolic mesentery, revealing the previously dissected plane for further visualization.
The surgeon completes additional dissection through the window created in the iliocolic mesentery, further exposing the previously dissected plane.
The surgeon widens the window in the iliocolic mesentery by taking the small bowel mesentery, continuing with an inferior approach to enhance visibility of the dissected plane.
The surgeon thins the adipose tissue over the junction of the superior mesenteric artery and vein with the iliocolic vessels, revealing these structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon marks the planned transection site on the colon, indicating the area where the surgical cut will be made.
The video shows a tumor located in the ascending colon.
A tattoo is visible at the hepatic flexure, located approximately five to six centimeters distal to the colon.
The surgeon marks the pathway for a formal right hemicolectomy, indicating the planned surgical approach.
The surgeon focuses on the junction where the iliocolic vessels join the superior mesenteric vessels, honing in on this anatomical area for precise identification.
The surgeon approaches the superior mesenteric vessels, positioning the instrument directly on top of the vessel at the iliocolic junction.

The surgeon is working near the iliocolic junction, where the iliocolic vessels join the superior mesenteric vessels, and is losing some clearance of nodal tissue on the anterior and left side of these vessels.
The surgeon identifies the ileocolic artery, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues to mobilize the mesentery, lifting it over the head of the pancreas, revealing the anatomical structures beneath.
The surgeon identifies the right colic artery branching from the ileocolic artery, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon observes that there is no true right colic artery branching from the superior mesenteric vessels.
The surgeon approaches the middle colic artery and identifies the right branch of the middle colic artery during the dissection.
The surgeon carefully exposes the blue-colored superior mesenteric vein, improving visibility and access during the procedure.
The surgeon elevates tissue from the undersurface of the superior mesenteric vein using an inferior approach, clearing the lateral surface of the vein.
The surgeon approaches the middle colic artery, tracing towards the right branch of the middle colic artery, with visible anatomical structures surrounding the area.
The surgeon is seen dissecting around the right branch of the middle colic artery, with the superior mesenteric vein visible in the background.
The surgeon identifies a low bifurcation of the middle colic artery, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon isolates and takes the right branch of the middle colic artery, using surgical instruments to carefully separate it from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies the middle colic vein, visually distinguishing it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon continues to dissect the T-colon mesentery, advancing towards the bowel wall at the planned distal colon margin.
The surgeon splits the omentum at the level of the planned colon margin, ensuring the omentum remains attached to the specimen.
The surgeon dissects through the gastrohepatic omentum to connect with the previously completed medial dissection, visible in the surgical field.
Adhesions between the omentum and gallbladder are visible, restricting the surgeon's ability to mobilize the colon.
The surgeon carefully dissects a portion of the middle colic vein, separating it from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon continues the dissection through the remaining tissue, advancing towards the planned distal colon margin, ensuring the omentum remains with the specimen.
The surgeon checks the proximity of the antrum and duodenum during the dissection, ensuring these structures are not inadvertently approached while continuing the procedure.
The surgeon navigates around the hepatic flexure, continuing the dissection to separate the colon from surrounding structures.
The surgeon addresses the remaining attachments of the right colon, visually separating them from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon checks the colon margin and prepares to apply a surgical stapler to secure the tissue.
The surgeon operates through a suprapubic port, which will be upsized for specimen extraction, minimizing the risk of hernia and requiring sutures for closure at only one incision site.
The surgeon is grasping and manipulating a segment of the small bowel, preparing it for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon places the resected specimen above the liver for temporary storage during the procedure.
The surgeon inspects the dissection area, examining the surgical site for any remaining tissue attachments or irregularities.
The superior mesenteric vein is visible adjacent to the head of the pancreas.
The surgeon aligns the bowel segments in preparation for a sewn anastomosis, ensuring precise positioning for the upcoming suturing process.
The surgeon switches to a different camera angle, positioning it in view two, and continues the procedure using two right-handed instruments.
The surgeon performs a collotomy, creating an incision in the colon wall.
The surgeon creates an enterotomy, visibly opening the intestinal wall.
The surgeon prepares to suture the enterotomy or collotomy site closed, using a needle and suture material to approximate the edges of the incision.
The surgeon begins suturing in the middle of the incision, progressing outward towards the far side.
The surgeon turns to perform the canal over the anterior row, indicating a shift in focus to this specific area during the procedure.
The surgeon switches to a different suture, starting in the middle of the surgical site, and continues suturing towards the near side.
The surgeon maneuvers around the corner of the surgical site, preparing to transition to the canal section of the procedure.
The surgeon cannels over the top towards the center, aiming to join the two sutures together at the anastomosis site.
The surgeon carefully tucks a small portion of mucosa back into the surgical site, ensuring it is properly positioned within the tissue layers.
The surgeon completes the suturing by bringing the original suture down over the top, ensuring the closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon ties two sutures together, completing the connection at the transition point of the canal.
The surgeon takes the original suture used for the back layer and brings it over the top, completing the two-layer anastomosis.
The surgeon inspects the mesenteric defect and places a portion of omentum into the defect without closing it.
The surgical specimen is extracted through the suprapubic incision, which has been modified to facilitate removal.
